data.title: AsiaFIN shows 50% topline growth in 2025
data.description:
• AsiaFIN (ASFH) FY 2025 Net loss was ~$120K for the year ended December 31, 2025 vs. $162K last year.

  • Revenue for the years ended December 31, 2025 and 2024 was approximately $5.126M and $3.382M, respectively. This represents a growth of 51.5% in the year 2025.

  • Cash and cash equivalents were approximately $1.748M as of December 31, 2025.

  • "Our Fintech business has recorded revenue growth of 52% from 2024, showing that the cheque clearing business can still deliver growth in our new markets. Our core revenue-generating unit, RegTech, continues to grow well, with gross margins improving by 13.46%. As we enter 2026, we are seeing traction in our Fintech and RegTech businesses whilst we are gaining new customers in our RPA business." said CEO Kai Cheong Wong.

data.title: A2Z Technologies reports FY results
data.description:
• A2Z Technologies press release (AZ): FY revenues of $7.9 million, compared to $5.4 million, for the Full-Year 2024.

  • As of December 31, 2025, the company's balance sheet included inventory of $ 3.9 million.

  • As of December 31, 2025, the company's balance sheet included working capital of $72.5 million.

data.title: Public Storage subsidiary prices $500M notes offering
data.description:
• Public Storage (PSA) subsidiary, Public Storage Operating Company, has priced a public offering of $500M of fixed-rate senior notes due 2035.
  • The notes will be issued at 99.182% of par value, bear interest at 5.000%, and will mature on December 15, 2035.
  • The offering is expected to close on April 6, 2026, subject to the satisfaction of customary closing conditions.
  • Public Storage Operating Company expects to use the net proceeds to repay amounts under its revolving credit facility and for general corporate purposes.
  • BofA Securities (BAC) and J.P. Morgan Securities (JPM) acted as joint book-running managers of the offering.

data.title: INOVIO Pharmaceuticals proposes securities offering
data.description:
• INOVIO Pharmaceuticals (INO) plans to sell common shares and accompanying Series A warrants and Series B warrants (or pre-funded warrants to purchase its common stock in lieu thereof), in an underwritten public offering.
  • The company will also give underwriters a 30-day option to buy up to 15% more shares and warrants. The offering is subject to market conditions, and its size and timing are not yet finalized.

data.title: IBM, Arm partner on enterprise computing
data.description:
IBM (IBM) has entered into a strategic collaboration with Arm (ARM) to develop new dual‑architecture hardware that helps enterprises run future AI and data intensive workloads.

The partnership is focused on three key areas. First, the companies are exploring how to expand virtualization technologies that allow Arm-based software environments to operate within IBM's enterprise computing platforms. This work is designed to expand software compatibility and further streamline how developers and enterprises bring Arm applications into mission-critical environments.

The companies are also exploring new ways to support the performance and efficiency demands of modern workloads, including AI and data intensive applications. The work includes enabling enterprise systems to recognize and execute Arm applications.

Finally, the collaboration is focused on long term ecosystem growth. By creating shared technology layers between platforms, IBM and Arm aim to open the door to broader software ecosystems and greater flexibility in how applications are deployed and managed.

Source: Press Release

data.title: Celldex prices $300M stock at $29 per share to fuel Barzolvolimab launch
Asian markets retreat as Trump’s 'nearing completion' address signals near-term escalation
data.description:
Asian equity markets traded lower on Thursday as investor sentiment weakened due to mixed signals from US President Donald Trump about the conflict in the Middle East. Trump stated in a national address that the war in Iran was “very close” to completing its goals, but he also warned of possible escalation in military actions while diplomatic efforts continue. He did not provide a clear timeline for the conflict's end.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) slipped about 2.0% to $66,512 on Thursday after U.S. President Trump struck a hawkish tone in a national address on the Iran conflict.

Gold prices declined to around $4,690 per ounce on Thursday, ending a four-day gain.

WTI crude futures jumped more than 5% on Thursday, reclaiming levels above $100 per barrel and halting a two-day decline.

The benchmark KOSPI slumped 3.87% to around 5,268 on Thursday, reversing the previous session’s gains as renewed geopolitical concerns rattled investors. The South Korean won depreciated past 1,520 per dollar, its lowest since March 2009.

Japan (NKY:IND) fell 2.65% to around 52,700, while the broader Topix Index slipped 0.9% to 3,600 on Thursday. The Japanese yen slipped to around 159.2 per dollar on Thursday.

China's (SHCOMP) stock markets fell 0.85% , and the offshore yuan weakened to around 6.88 per dollar, ending a three-day winning streak.

The People's Bank of China (PBoC) withdrew cash from the financial system for the first time in a year, a cautious signal that keeps its policy options open as higher oil prices filter through the economy, as reported by Bloomberg. 

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 1.46% to 25,082 on Thursday, extending losses for a second straight session.

India (SENSEX) fell 1.95% to below 71,700 on Thursday, after a strong 1.8% gain in the previous session. The Indian rupee edged down to around 93.2 per dollar, extending gains for another session.

The HSBC India Manufacturing PMI fell to 53.9 in March 2026 from 56.9 in February, slightly higher than preliminary estimates of 53.8.

Australia (AS51) fell 1.44% to 8,707 in early Thursday trade, extending gains for a third session. The Australian dollar closed at 8,573 on Thursday, erasing early gains and snapping a two-session gain.

Australia's trade surplus rose to AUD 5.69 billion in February 2026, up from AUD 2.26 billion in January, surpassing expectations of AUD 2.6 billion, boosted by gold and farm exports.

In the U.S. on Wednesday, all three major indexes ended higher as growing optimism for a resolution to the Middle East conflict fueled a second day of gains to start the month

Trading wraps Thursday ahead of the Good Friday closure, with jobless claims in focus before March’s payrolls report Friday.

U.S. stock futures fell sharply on Thursday after President Donald Trump warned in a national address that Washington would “hit” Iran “extremely hard” within two to three weeks: Dow -1.09%; S&P 500 -1.26%; Nasdaq -1.64%.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

More on Asia's markets and economy:

data.title: Millicom prices reopening of $75M senior notes
data.description:
• Millicom International Cellular (TIGO) priced the reopening of an additional $75M of its 7.375% senior notes due 2032 at 100.985% of par, plus accrued and unpaid interest from the prior issue’s interest‑payment date.
  • The offering adds to an existing $450M tranche issued in 2024.
  • The interest on the notes is payable semi-annually in arrears on April 2 and October 2 of each year and they will mature on April 2, 2032, unless earlier repurchased or redeemed.
  • Millicom (TIGO) intends to use net proceeds from this reopening primarily for refinancing or repaying existing indebtedness and for general corporate purposes.

data.title: Trump threatens to hit Iran ‘extremely hard’ even as war nears end; oil surges, futures dip
data.description:
President Donald Trump said the U.S. will strike Iran “extremely hard” over the next two to three weeks, while signaling the conflict could be short-lived and that talks with Tehran remain ongoing, keeping the door open for a diplomatic resolution.

In a rare prime-time address on Wednesday, President Donald Trump portrayed the Iran war as nearing success, saying U.S. forces had largely achieved their objectives, including crippling Iran’s missile capabilities and degrading its air force, navy and industrial base. He said the campaign would weaken Tehran’s proxies and block its path to nuclear weapons.

“Tonight I’m pleased to say that these core strategic objectives are nearing completion,” Trump said from the White House in a roughly 20-minute speech. “We are going to finish the job. and we’re going to finish it very fast, we’re getting very close.”

Trump signaled a potential escalation in military action, saying the U.S. could intensify strikes over the next two to three weeks and warning Iran would be pushed “back to the stone ages.”

At the same time, he left the door open to diplomacy, saying talks are ongoing. However, he cautioned that if no deal is reached, the U.S. could “hit each and every one of their electric generating plants very hard and probably simultaneously.”

Secretary of State Marco Rubio echoed Trump’s message, outlining what the administration described as its core objectives in Iran: dismantling the country’s weapons production, degrading its navy and air force, and ensuring it cannot develop nuclear weapons.

Trump’s remarks unsettled investors. U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures (CL1:COM) gained 4.7% to $104.21 a barrel. ET. International benchmark Brent crude futures (CO1:COM) rose 5.9% to $107.1 per barrel.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

Treasury yields rose, with the benchmark U.S. 10 Year Treasury yield (US10Y) up 1.4% to 4.38%. U.S. equity futures pulled back. S&P 500 futures (SPX) fell 1.1% to 6,501, while Dow futures (INDU) dropped -0.9% to 46,122. Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) fell -1.5% to 23,652.7.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion

data.title: FitLife outlines Irwin Amazon run rate of $9M to $10M while holding off on 2026 guidance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: FitLife Brands (FTLF) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Dayton Judd said Q4 was “the first full quarter that includes the financial results for Irwin Naturals,” and framed 2025 performance as “a strong year for all of our brand groupings other than MRC.” He reported legacy FitLife (excluding MRC and MusclePharm) delivered “organic revenue growth of approximately 6%,” with “online revenue… increased approximately 16%,” while “MRC revenue declined approximately 15%.”
  • Judd detailed Irwin “normalized” revenue after the loss of Costco U.S., Rite Aid, and the CBD exit, stating: “Irwin’s net revenue for the full year of 2024 would have been $54 million, and its revenue for the full year of 2025 would have been $54 million,” and for Q4 (first full quarter owned) “Irwin delivered organic growth of approximately 6% compared to the fourth quarter of 2024.”
  • Judd reported Q4 consolidated results and mix: “Total revenue was $25.9 million,” with “wholesale revenue… $15.5 million” and “online revenue… $10.5 million.” He added, “Excluding the amortization of the inventory step-up… gross margin was 37.0%,” and “Adjusted EBITDA was $3.5 million.”
  • Judd highlighted legacy FitLife weakness, saying it “accelerated late in the fourth quarter and into the first quarter,” and reported legacy FitLife Q4 revenue of “$13.3 million,” with a “12% decrease in total revenue.”
  • Judd emphasized Irwin’s Amazon ramp: “approximately $60,000 of revenue in October, $300,000… in November and almost $500,000… in December,” adding that by Q1 2026 monthly revenue was “approximately $0.8 million,” which he characterized as “roughly $9 million to $10 million of annual revenue on a run rate basis.”
  • No CFO prepared-remarks quote was included in the transcript; CEO Judd noted CFO Jakob York attended the call.

Outlook

  • Judd said, “we are going to hold off on providing any kind of formal guidance at this point in time,” citing “weakness in the first quarter” and uncertainty around “how long the exogenous challenges will persist and how quickly our internal efforts will bear fruit.”
  • In Q&A, Judd addressed the prior acquisition framework referenced by an analyst, noting the combined business at the end of 2025 was “about $116 million,” and said, “Q1 is not better than Q4… we’re pacing a little bit down in Q1 compared to Q4,” later reiterating, “Q1 looks a whole lot like Q4.”
  • Judd outlined gross margin direction qualitatively: “40% is probably a stretch,” but he said he believed the combined business could move toward “something closer to the high 30%” over time as Irwin supply chain initiatives progress.
  • Analysts’ estimates were provided, but management issued no EPS or revenue guidance in the current transcript to compare against those estimates.

Financial Results

  • Judd reported Q4 net income and key items: “Net income for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $1.6 million compared to $2.1 million,” attributing the decline “primarily by transaction-related expense and amortization of the inventory step-up.”
  • Judd said wholesale growth reflected Irwin: wholesale revenue rose “213% compared to the fourth quarter of 2024,” while online revenue increased “4% compared to the fourth quarter of 2024.”
  • On brand profitability, Judd stated Irwin Q4 gross margin was “28.0%,” and “Adjusting for the amortization of the inventory step-up, Irwin’s gross margin… would have been 33.2%,” with contribution margin “26.6%” or “31.8%” adjusted.
  • On acquisition accounting, Judd told an analyst: “It is done… the last expensing of that was in Q4,” adding, “in the Q1 numbers and beyond, you will not see any amortization of inventory step-up.”

Q&A

  • Ryan Meyers, Lake Street: Asked whether Amazon vs. macro was the bigger headwind; CEO Judd replied, “I don’t have a good answer. I don’t know how to bifurcate them,” adding category growth “flipped negative” recently while company declines were larger, and out-of-stocks were “definitely in the hundreds of thousands.”
  • Meyers, Lake Street: Asked about gross margin normalization; CEO Judd said, “40% is probably a stretch,” and over time “something closer to the high 30% is reasonable.”
  • Samir Patel, Askeladden: Pressed on the previously discussed $120 million revenue and $20 million to $25 million adjusted EBITDA concept; CEO Judd said he would provide guidance “if I had any confidence,” and reiterated Q1 was tracking like Q4.
  • Patel, Askeladden: Asked why exit CBD; CEO Judd said, “margin would be the reason to keep it,” but “CBD is an incredibly complex as it relates to the legal environment,” and after a federal spending bill, it became “certainly very difficult to legally sell CBD.”
  • Patel, Askeladden: Asked timing for 3-year dating; CEO Judd said, “you’ll start to see the impact… in Q2 and beyond,” explaining it requires reformulation and testing, and would show as “lower charge-off to inventory.”
  • Sean McGowan, ROTH: Asked about inventory step-up; CEO Judd answered, “It is done.” McGowan also questioned the shelf-life fix; Judd said, “this is not easy… but it’s unequivocally worth the effort,” and explained use of “retains” for testing.
  • McGowan, ROTH: Asked for Irwin’s adjusted Q1 2025 revenue baseline; CEO Judd said, “adjusted net revenue taking out Costco U.S. CBD and Rite Aid was $14.3 million in Q1 of 2025.”
  • McGowan, ROTH: Asked about MusclePharm remedy; CEO Judd cited protein input costs, stating “Third quarter protein is now $11 a pound,” and said he “turned down probably $1.5 million” of low-margin international orders to “protect margin.”
  • James Bogan, Legends Capital: Challenged MusclePharm rebuild expectations; CEO Judd said, “anyone that has any expectation that this is going to be $175 million brand again? I would just encourage you to temper… enthusiasm,” and added, “I will never buy another brand that is IP only, and I will never buy another brand that is protein-dominant.”
  • 2by2 Capital (indiscernible): Asked about Costco U.S. relisting; CEO Judd said, “you should not plan on us getting back in there anytime soon,” while noting Costco Canada remained active.
  • Tyler Hill (private investor): Asked about off-Amazon strategy and MRC retention; CEO Judd said, “Our challenge has not been retention… It’s really just it’s traffic,” and described “centralized marketing,” more “e-mail marketing,” “Shopify,” and social ads.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly negative to skeptical, focusing on durability of the acquisition plan, Q1 softness, margins, and brand challenges, including Patel’s framing around the earlier $120 million/$20 million-$25 million concept and Bogan’s push on MusclePharm’s rebuild.
  • Management tone was neutral to slightly defensive on near-term demand, with Judd repeatedly emphasizing uncertainty and declining to quantify outcomes: “I don’t have a great answer,” and “because I don’t know, I don’t want to tell you guys what’s going to happen.”
  • Compared with Q3, management’s prepared-remarks caution became more explicit in Q4, shifting from “To be clear, the sky is not falling” (Q3) to “hold off on providing any kind of formal guidance” (Q4) and confirming Q1 weakness similar to Q4.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q4 introduced a clearer downturn in near-term commentary, with Judd saying weakness “accelerated late in the fourth quarter and into the first quarter,” versus Q3’s earlier-stage “cracks in September” and “October was a bit soft.”
  • Q4 confirmed the inventory step-up amortization ended (“the last expensing… was in Q4”), whereas Q3 indicated a remaining balance would “all flow through in the fourth quarter.”
  • Q4 provided more detail on Irwin normalization (removing Costco U.S., Rite Aid, CBD) and highlighted Amazon scaling from launch, while Q3 described early Amazon revenue at “approximately $10,000 of revenue daily.”
  • Analyst focus shifted from Q3’s emphasis on subscriber declines and whey-protein-driven margin pressure to Q4’s emphasis on bifurcating macro vs. Amazon algorithm impacts, the absence of 2026 guidance, and how Irwin’s online ramp could offset broader weakness.

Risks and Concerns

  • Judd cited “economic and political volatility,” “consumer confidence concerns,” and said “consumer discretionary spending has been declining since late last year.”
  • Judd identified operational risk at Irwin including “stockouts… particularly pronounced during the first quarter,” and stated Irwin historically disposed of “approximately $2 million of obsolete inventory every year,” which he said “has the potential to increase Irwin’s gross margins by as much as 300 to 400 basis points” if reduced.
  • On platform risk, Judd attributed Amazon softness to algorithm changes (A9 to “A10”), stating the issue was “traffic and not conversion,” and described a portfolio pattern: “the more dependent the brand is on Amazon, the more it is struggling on the platform.”
  • On regulatory risk, Judd said CBD’s legal complexity and federal changes made it “not worth the complexity,” leading to discontinuation, with remaining inventory expected to clear “later in 2026.”

Final Takeaway

FitLife’s Q4 call centered on Irwin Naturals’ first full-quarter contribution and a sharper near-term slowdown across legacy brands, with CEO Dayton Judd confirming Q1 trends looked “a whole lot like Q4” and choosing to withhold formal 2026 guidance. Management highlighted Irwin’s rapidly scaling Amazon channel—now described as a $9 million to $10 million annualized run rate—and outlined operational levers (3-year dating, reduced obsolescence, improved supply chain, and centralized marketing) intended to lift margins and stabilize demand amid macro pressure and Amazon traffic headwinds.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
